response: A balance sheet is a financial statement that shows what a company owns (assets), owes (liabilities), and the residual value for owners (equity) at a specific point in time. The fundamental equation is: Assets = Liabilities + Equity.

An income statement (also called a P&L) shows revenue earned and expenses incurred over a period. Revenue minus expenses equals net income — the bottom line that tells you whether the business is profitable.

The cash flow statement tracks how cash moves in and out of a business across three categories: operating activities (day-to-day), investing activities (buying/selling assets), and financing activities (debt and equity transactions).

A trial balance lists all accounts and their balances at a point in time. Total debits should equal total credits — if they do, your books are in balance. It's the starting point for preparing financial statements.

Double-entry bookkeeping means every transaction affects at least two accounts — a debit and a credit. Debits increase assets and expenses; credits increase liabilities, equity, and revenue. The system ensures books always balance.
GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) is the standard framework for financial reporting in the US. It ensures consistency, comparability, and transparency across financial statements.

Accrual basis recognizes revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash timing. Cash basis only records transactions when cash changes hands. Most businesses use accrual for more accurate financial reporting.

Depreciation spreads the cost of a long-term asset over its useful life. Common methods include straight-line (equal amounts each period) and declining balance (more in early years). It's a non-cash expense that reduces taxable income.

Amortization has two meanings:

1. Spreading an intangible asset's cost over its useful life
2. The gradual repayment of a loan through scheduled payments of principal and interest
Accounts Payable (AP) tracks money your business owes to vendors and suppliers. When you receive a bill, it increases AP (a liability). When you pay it, AP decreases and cash decreases.
Accounts Receivable (AR) tracks money owed to your business by customers. When you send an invoice, AR increases (an asset). When the customer pays, AR decreases and cash increases.

Equity represents the owners' residual interest in the business after liabilities are subtracted from assets. It includes contributed capital, retained earnings (accumulated profits), and owner draws.
A journal entry records a business transaction in the accounting system. Each entry has a date, accounts affected, debit and credit amounts, and a memo. Debits must equal credits.
The General Ledger is the master record of all financial transactions, organized by account. Every journal entry posts to the GL, which feeds the trial balance and financial statements.
The Chart of Accounts is an organized list of every account in your accounting system, grouped by type: assets, liabilities, equity, revenue, and expenses. Each account has a number and name.
Break-even analysis tells you how much revenue or how many units you need to sell to cover all costs. At the break-even point, total revenue equals total costs — no profit, no loss.
The Time Value of Money (TVM) principle states that a dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future. TVM calculations help compare investments, loans, and financial decisions across time.
Markup is the percentage added to cost to get the selling price. Margin is the percentage of the selling price that is profit. A 50% markup equals a 33% margin — they're related but not the same!

Financial ratios analyze a company's performance and health. Key categories include liquidity (current ratio), profitability (ROE, net margin), solvency (debt-to-equity), and efficiency (asset turnover).

Under ASC 842, leases are classified as either operating or finance. The classification depends on factors like lease term, present value of payments, and whether ownership transfers. Both types go on the balance sheet.
An aging report groups outstanding invoices or bills by how long they've been unpaid: Current, 1-30 days, 31-60 days, 61-90 days, and 90+ days. It helps prioritize collections and manage cash flow.
You can record and manage vendor bills in the AP Management module. Enter bill details, track due dates, and record payments — all with automatic GL posting.

The ASC (Accounting Standards Codification) is the authoritative source of US GAAP, organized by topic. Veracity includes a browsable reference of key ASC topics.

Adjusting entries are journal entries made at the end of an accounting period to update account balances before preparing financial statements. Common types include accruals (recording earned revenue or incurred expenses not yet recorded), deferrals (allocating prepaid items), and estimates (like depreciation or bad debt).

Closing entries zero out temporary accounts (revenue, expenses, dividends) at period end, transferring their balances to retained earnings. This resets the income statement for the next period. The sequence is: close revenue to income summary, close expenses to income summary, close income summary to retained earnings, close dividends to retained earnings.

A bank reconciliation compares your book balance to the bank statement balance, identifying differences like outstanding checks, deposits in transit, bank fees, and errors. The goal is to arrive at an adjusted balance that matches on both sides.

Working capital is current assets minus current liabilities. It measures short-term liquidity — whether a business can cover its near-term obligations. Positive working capital means the company has enough liquid assets to pay upcoming bills.

The current ratio is current assets divided by current liabilities. A ratio above 1.0 means the company can cover short-term debts. A ratio of 2.0 is often considered healthy, but the ideal varies by industry.

FIFO (First In, First Out) assumes the oldest inventory is sold first — better in rising prices for lower COGS. LIFO (Last In, First Out) assumes newest inventory is sold first — higher COGS, lower taxes. Weighted average cost uses the average cost of all units. LIFO is not allowed under IFRS.

Bad debt expense accounts for receivables that won't be collected. The allowance method estimates uncollectible amounts in advance (contra-asset account), while the direct write-off method records bad debt only when specific accounts are deemed uncollectible. GAAP requires the allowance method.

Unearned revenue is a liability — money received from customers before delivering goods or services. As you fulfill the obligation, you recognize revenue and reduce the liability. Common examples: prepaid subscriptions, retainers, gift cards.

Prepaid expenses are assets — payments made in advance for future benefits. As the benefit is consumed (e.g., a month of insurance passes), you record an adjusting entry to move the amount from the asset to an expense account.

Accrued expenses are liabilities for costs incurred but not yet paid or recorded. Common examples: wages earned by employees but not yet paid, interest that has accumulated on a loan, utilities consumed but not yet billed.

Payroll involves calculating gross pay, withholding taxes (federal, state, FICA), and employer-side taxes (Social Security, Medicare, FUTA, SUTA). The journal entry debits wage expense and payroll tax expense, credits cash and various tax payable accounts.
Sales tax collected from customers is a liability (Sales Tax Payable), not revenue — you're collecting on behalf of the government. When you remit the tax, you debit the payable and credit cash.

Fixed assets (PP&E) are long-term tangible assets used in operations: buildings, equipment, vehicles, furniture. They're recorded at cost and depreciated over their useful life. Repairs are expensed; improvements are capitalized.

A contra account offsets its parent account. Accumulated Depreciation (contra-asset) reduces the book value of fixed assets. Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (contra-asset) reduces accounts receivable. Sales Returns (contra-revenue) reduces gross revenue.

A subsidiary ledger provides detail behind a general ledger control account. The AP subledger shows individual vendor balances that sum to total Accounts Payable. The AR subledger shows individual customer balances that sum to total Accounts Receivable.

Month-end close is the process of finalizing all transactions for the period: post all entries, reconcile bank accounts, record adjusting entries (accruals, deferrals, depreciation), review the trial balance, and prepare financial statements. A checklist helps ensure nothing is missed.

Year-end close includes everything in a monthly close plus: close all temporary accounts to retained earnings, prepare annual financial statements, review for audit readiness, file tax returns, and set up the new fiscal year. It's the most comprehensive close cycle.

A financial audit is an independent examination of financial statements to verify they're fairly presented according to GAAP. Auditors test internal controls, sample transactions, confirm balances, and issue an opinion. A clean audit opinion means the statements are materially correct.

Internal controls are policies and procedures that safeguard assets, ensure accurate records, and prevent fraud. Key principles include segregation of duties (no one person controls an entire transaction), authorization requirements, physical safeguards, and regular reconciliations.

Materiality is the threshold at which information could influence a financial decision. An error is material if it would change a user's judgment. It applies to both individual items and aggregates. Auditors set materiality thresholds to focus on what matters most.

Under ASC 606, revenue is recognized in five steps:

1. Identify the contract
2. Identify performance obligations
3. Determine the transaction price
4. Allocate the price to obligations
5. Recognize revenue as obligations are satisfied

Revenue is recognized when control transfers to the customer.

The matching principle requires expenses to be recorded in the same period as the revenues they help generate. If you sell a product in March, the cost of goods sold is recorded in March too — not when you bought the inventory or when the customer pays.

Going concern assumes a business will continue operating for the foreseeable future. If there's substantial doubt about this (e.g., severe losses, inability to pay debts), it must be disclosed. Auditors evaluate going concern status during every audit.

The conservatism principle (prudence) says: when in doubt, choose the option that understates assets/income and overstates liabilities/expenses. Recognize losses as soon as they're probable, but only recognize gains when they're realized. It prevents overly optimistic reporting.

A fiscal year is any 12-month period used for financial reporting. It can match the calendar year (Jan-Dec) or end on any date that makes sense for the business (e.g., Jun 30 for schools, Sep 30 for government). The IRS requires consistency once chosen.

A petty cash fund is a small amount of cash kept on hand for minor expenses (office supplies, postage, small reimbursements). It's established by debiting Petty Cash and crediting Cash. When replenished, you debit the expense accounts and credit Cash.

Bank fees (service charges, NSF fees, wire fees) are discovered during bank reconciliation. They reduce the book balance and are recorded as: debit Bank Fees Expense (or Miscellaneous Expense), credit Cash.

Deposits in transit are amounts recorded in your books but not yet on the bank statement — they increase the bank balance. Outstanding checks are checks you've written but the bank hasn't processed yet — they decrease the bank balance. Both are normal reconciling items.

The journal (or book of original entry) is where transactions are first recorded in chronological order before being posted to the general ledger. Each journal entry includes a date, accounts, debit/credit amounts, and a description.

Posting is the process of transferring journal entry information to the general ledger accounts. Each debit and credit from the journal is recorded in the corresponding ledger account, updating the running balance.

The accounting equation is: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Every transaction must keep this equation in balance. It's the foundation of double-entry bookkeeping and the structure behind the balance sheet.

Normal balance is the side (debit or credit) that increases an account. Assets and expenses have debit normal balances. Liabilities, equity, and revenue have credit normal balances. Knowing normal balances helps you quickly spot errors.

A T-account is a visual representation of a ledger account shaped like the letter T. Debits go on the left side, credits on the right. It's a quick way to analyze how transactions affect individual accounts.

Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) represents the direct costs of producing goods that were sold: raw materials, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead. It appears on the income statement and is subtracted from revenue to calculate gross profit.

Gross profit is revenue minus cost of goods sold. It shows how much money is left after covering direct production costs, before operating expenses. Gross margin (gross profit / revenue) is a key profitability metric.

Operating expenses are the costs of running the business beyond direct production: rent, salaries, marketing, insurance, utilities, office supplies. They're subtracted from gross profit to arrive at operating income.

Net income is the bottom line — total revenue minus all expenses (COGS, operating expenses, interest, taxes). It flows to the balance sheet as an increase to retained earnings and represents the company's profitability for the period.

Retained earnings is the cumulative total of net income minus dividends paid over the life of the business. It represents profits that have been reinvested rather than distributed to owners. It's an equity account on the balance sheet.

Dividends are distributions of profits to shareholders (corporations) or owner draws (sole proprietors/partnerships). They reduce retained earnings and cash but are NOT expenses — they're a return of equity to owners.

There are five main account types:

1. Assets — what the business owns
2. Liabilities — what the business owes
3. Equity — owner's residual interest
4. Revenue — income earned
5. Expenses — costs incurred

The first three are balance sheet accounts; the last two are income statement accounts.

Return on Investment (ROI) measures profitability: (Net Profit / Cost of Investment) x 100. An ROI of 20% means you earned $0.20 for every $1 invested. It's one of the most widely used metrics for evaluating investments.
Return on Equity (ROE) is net income divided by shareholders' equity. It measures how effectively a company uses equity to generate profits. Higher ROE means better returns for investors.

Net Present Value (NPV) is the sum of all future cash flows discounted back to today, minus the initial investment. A positive NPV means the investment creates value; negative means it destroys value. It's the gold standard for capital budgeting decisions.

The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate that makes a project's NPV equal to zero. If the IRR exceeds your required rate of return (hurdle rate), the investment is worthwhile. It's useful for comparing projects of different sizes.

WACC is the blended cost of a company's financing — the weighted average of the cost of debt and cost of equity. It's used as the discount rate in NPV calculations. A lower WACC means cheaper financing and higher project values.

Capital budgeting is the process of evaluating long-term investments: new equipment, expansion, acquisitions. Key techniques include NPV, IRR, payback period, and profitability index. The goal is to invest in projects that create value above the cost of capital.

The cash conversion cycle measures how long it takes to convert inventory investment into cash: Days Inventory Outstanding + Days Sales Outstanding - Days Payable Outstanding. A shorter cycle means faster cash generation.

The debt-to-equity ratio is total liabilities divided by total equity. It measures financial leverage — how much the company relies on debt versus equity financing. Higher ratios mean more risk but potentially higher returns.

Compound interest earns interest on both the principal and previously accumulated interest. The formula is: FV = PV x (1 + r)^n. Over time, compounding dramatically accelerates growth — Einstein allegedly called it the eighth wonder of the world.

Simple interest is calculated only on the original principal: Interest = Principal x Rate x Time. Unlike compound interest, it doesn't earn interest on interest. It's used for some short-term loans and bonds.

An annuity is a series of equal payments at regular intervals — like a car payment or mortgage. Present value of an annuity calculates what those future payments are worth today; future value calculates what they'll grow to.

A bond is a debt instrument where the issuer promises to pay periodic interest (coupon) and return the face value at maturity. Bonds trade at a premium (above par) when market rates are below the coupon rate, and at a discount when above.

Interest rates represent the cost of borrowing money. The Federal Reserve sets the federal funds rate, which influences all other rates. A basis point is 1/100th of a percent. Rising rates increase borrowing costs and can reduce asset values.

To reconcile an account:

1. Get the ending balance from both sources (bank and books)
2. Add deposits in transit to the bank balance
3. Subtract outstanding checks from the bank balance
4. Record bank adjustments (fees, interest) in your books
5. Verify both adjusted balances match

Month-end close checklist:

1. Post all transactions
2. Reconcile bank accounts
3. Record adjusting entries (accruals, depreciation)
4. Review AP/AR aging
5. Run trial balance and verify it balances
6. Prepare financial statements
7. Archive supporting docs

To set up a Chart of Accounts:

1. Choose a numbering system (e.g., 1000s = assets, 2000s = liabilities)
2. Add accounts for each category your business needs
3. Keep it simple — start with essentials and add as needed
4. Match it to your industry

Veracity includes industry templates!

Start with the income statement (are we profitable?), then the balance sheet (what do we own and owe?), then cash flow (where is cash going?). Compare periods to spot trends. Calculate key ratios. Look for red flags: declining margins, rising debt, negative cash flow from operations.

For a sales return: debit Sales Returns & Allowances (contra-revenue) and credit Accounts Receivable (or Cash if already paid). If inventory is returned to stock, also debit Inventory and credit COGS. This properly reduces revenue without hiding the gross sales figure.

When receiving a loan: debit Cash, credit Notes Payable (or Long-Term Debt). For each payment: debit Interest Expense (for the interest portion) and debit Notes Payable (for the principal portion), credit Cash for the total payment.

To record an asset purchase: debit the asset account (Equipment, Vehicle, etc.) for the full cost including delivery, installation, and setup, then credit Cash or Accounts Payable. The asset then gets depreciated over its useful life.

Tips received by employees are taxable income. The business records tip income as part of payroll, withholds appropriate taxes, and reports on W-2s. Cash tips must be reported by employees if they exceed $20/month.

Two main systems: Perpetual (updates inventory continuously with each sale/purchase — most common with modern software) and Periodic (counts inventory at period end to calculate COGS). The journal entry to record a sale under perpetual: debit COGS and credit Inventory, plus debit AR/Cash and credit Revenue.

A 1099-NEC is issued to independent contractors paid $600+ in a year. Unlike W-2 employees, contractors handle their own taxes. The business deducts the payment as an expense but doesn't withhold taxes. 1099s are due to recipients by January 31.

A W-2 is issued to employees by January 31, showing total wages, tips, and tax withholdings for the year. Employers file copies with the Social Security Administration. It's the basis for the employee's personal tax return.

Estimated taxes are quarterly payments made by self-employed individuals and businesses to cover income tax not withheld by an employer. Due dates: April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15. Underpayment can result in penalties.

Tax deductions reduce taxable income. Common business deductions: office rent, salaries, insurance, depreciation, supplies, travel, professional fees, and home office expenses. Deductions must be ordinary (common in your industry) and necessary (helpful for business).

Common entity types: Sole proprietorship (simplest, no separation from owner), LLC (liability protection, flexible taxation), S-Corp (pass-through taxation, salary requirement), C-Corp (separate entity, double taxation but unlimited growth potential). Each has different tax and legal implications.

General accounting best practices:

1. Reconcile accounts monthly
2. Record transactions promptly
3. Separate business and personal finances
4. Back up your data
5. Review financial statements regularly
6. Keep supporting documentation
7. Use a consistent chart of accounts

To correct an accounting error, never just delete the entry. Instead, record a reversing entry (debit what was credited, credit what was debited) to undo it cleanly, then record the correct entry. This maintains a complete audit trail.

Recommended frequencies:

• Bank reconciliation — monthly
• Financial statements — monthly
• AP/AR review — weekly
• Trial balance — monthly
• Full close — monthly (more thorough quarterly and annually)
• Tax filings — quarterly estimated, annual returns

For a new small business:

1. Choose your entity type (LLC is popular)
2. Open a separate business bank account
3. Set up a Chart of Accounts
4. Choose accrual or cash basis
5. Track every transaction from day one
6. Set aside money for taxes

Veracity can help with all of this!

A bookkeeper handles day-to-day transaction recording. An accountant prepares financial statements and provides strategic advice. A CPA (Certified Public Accountant) can perform audits and sign tax returns. Small businesses often start with a bookkeeper and add a CPA for tax season.

Veracity is designed to complement tools like QuickBooks, Xero, and FreshBooks by providing educational resources, practice tools, and analytical calculators. You can use Veracity to understand concepts, practice journal entries, and analyze financial ratios alongside your primary accounting software.

Profitability is measured at multiple levels: Gross profit (revenue - COGS), operating income (gross profit - operating expenses), and net income (after all expenses including tax). Check your Income Statement to see where you stand.

Cash flow problems often come from:

• Slow collections (AR aging too high)
• Overstocking inventory
• Excessive fixed costs
• Rapid growth outpacing cash

Solutions:
• Tighten payment terms
• Follow up on overdue invoices
• Negotiate longer vendor terms
• Build a cash reserve

Check your AP/AR aging report for red flags.

The model takes into account the asset's sensitivity to non-diversifiable risk (also known as systematic risk or market risk), often represented by the quantity beta (β) in the fina...

The price–earnings ratio, also known as P/E ratio, P/E, or PER, is the ratio of a company's share price to the company's earnings per share. The ratio is used for valuing companies and to find out whether they are overvalued or undervalued.

Enterprise value (EV), total enterprise value (TEV), or firm value (FV) is an economic measure reflecting the market value of a business (i.e. as distinct from market price). It is a sum of claims by all claimants: creditors (secured and unsecured) and shareholders (preferred and common). Enterprise value is one of the fundamental metrics used in business valuation, financial analysis, accounti...

Inventory (British English) or stock (American English) is a quantity of the goods and materials that a business holds for the ultimate goal of resale, production or utilisation.[nb 1] Inventory management is a discipline primarily about specifying the shape and placement of stocked goods. It is required at different locations within a facility or within many locations of a supply network to precede the regular and planned course of production and stock of materials.

Research and development (R&D or R+D) is the set of innovative activities undertaken by corporations, wealthy industrialists (such as Alfred Lee Loomis), or governments in developing new services or products. R&D constitutes the first stage of development of a potential new service or the production process. Although R&D activities may differ across businesses, the primary goal of an R&D department is to develop new products and services.

An Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) is a legal obligation associated with the retirement of a tangible long-lived asset in which the timing or method of settlement may be conditional on a future event, the occurrence of which may not be within the control of the entity burdened by the obligation. A treasury stock or reacquired stock is stock which is bought back by the issuing company, reducing the amount of outstanding stock on the open market ("open market" including insiders' holdings). Stock repurchases are used as a tax efficient method to put cash into shareholders' hands, rather than paying dividends, in jurisdictions that treat capital gains more favorably. A pension (/ˈpɛnʃən/; from Latin pensiō 'payment') is a fund into which amounts are paid regularly during an individual's working career, and from which periodic payments are made to support the person's retirement from work. A pension may be either a "defined benefit plan", where defined periodic payments are made in retirement and the contributions to the pension are adjusted to support these defined retirement payments, or a "defined contribution plan", under which defined amounts are paid...

A defined benefit (DB) pension plan is a type of pension plan in which an employer/sponsor promises a specified pension payment, lump-sum, or combination thereof on retirement that depends on an employee's earnings history, tenure of service and age, rather than depending directly on individual investment returns. Traditionally, many governmental and public entities, as well as a large number of corporations, provide defined benefit plans, sometimes as a means of compensating workers in lieu ...

Under U.S. Federal income tax law, a net operating loss (NOL) occurs when certain tax-deductible expenses exceed taxable revenues for a taxable year. If a taxpayer is taxed during profitable periods without receiving any tax relief (e.g., a refund) during periods of NOLs, an unbalanced tax burden results. Consequently, in some situations, Congress allows taxpayers to use the losses in one year to offset the profits of other years.

A variable interest entity (VIE) is a legal structure defined by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) for situations where control over a legal entity may be demonstrated through means other than voting rights. A public company with a financial interest in such entities may be subject to certain financial reporting requirements. VIEs gained notoriety in the early 2000s due to their role in the Enron scandal, where the company used special-purpose entities to hide mounting losses fr...

Foreign exchange risk (also known as FX risk, exchange rate risk or currency risk) is a financial risk that exists when a financial transaction is denominated in a currency other than the domestic currency of the company. The exchange risk arises when there is a risk of an unfavourable change in exchange rate between the domestic currency and the denominated currency before the date when the transaction is completed. Foreign exchange risk also exists when the foreign subsidiary of a firm main...

In trade, barter (derived from bareter) is a system of exchange in which participants in a transaction directly exchange goods or services for other goods or services without using a medium of exchange, such as money. Barter is considered one of the earliest systems of economic exchange, used before the invention of money. Economists usually distinguish barter from gift economies in many ways; barter, for example, features immediate reciprocal exchange, not one delayed in time. Barter usually...

Factoring is a financial transaction and a type of debtor finance in which a business sells its accounts receivable (i.e., invoices) to a third party (called a factor) at a discount. A business will sometimes factor its receivable assets to meet its present and immediate cash needs. Forfaiting is a factoring arrangement used in international trade finance by exporters who wish to sell their receivables to a forfaiter. Market capitalization, sometimes referred to as market cap, is the total value of a publicly traded company's outstanding common shares owned by stockholders. Market capitalization is equal to the market price per common share multiplied by the number of common shares outstanding.

In finance, the beta (β or market beta or beta coefficient) is a statistic that measures the expected increase or decrease of an individual stock price in proportion to movements of the stock market as a whole. Beta can be used to indicate the contribution of an individual asset to the market risk of a portfolio when it is added in small quantity. It refers to an asset's non-diversifiable risk, systematic risk, or market risk. Beta is not a measure of idiosyncratic risk. Beta is the hedge rat...

In finance and economics, systematic risk (in economics often called aggregate risk or undiversifiable risk) is vulnerability to events which affect aggregate outcomes such as broad market returns, total economy-wide resource holdings, or aggregate income. In many contexts, events like earthquakes, epidemics and major weather catastrophes pose aggregate risks that affect not only the distribution but also the total amount of resources. That is why it is also known as contingent risk, unplanne...

In finance, Jensen's alpha (or Jensen's Performance Index, ex-post alpha) is used to determine the abnormal return of a security or portfolio of securities over the theoretical expected return. It is a version of the standard alpha based on a theoretical performance instead of a market index. The security could be any asset, such as stocks, bonds, or derivatives. The theoretical return is predicted by a market model, most commonly the capital asset pricing model (CAPM). The market model uses ...

The Modigliani–Miller theorem (of Franco Modigliani, Merton Miller) is an influential element of economic theory; it forms the basis for modern thinking on capital structure. Reserve requirements are central bank regulations that set the minimum amount that a commercial bank must hold in liquid assets. This minimum amount, commonly referred to as the commercial bank's reserve, is generally determined by the central bank on the basis of a specified proportion of deposit liabilities of the bank. Duration (finance) is a measure of how the price of a fixed-income instrument responds to a change in interest rates. It is used to compare rate risk across bonds and to construct hedges, and is often paired with convexity and the price value of a basis point. Duration-based estimates work best for small, parallel shifts in the yield curve. In mathematical finance, convexity refers to non-linearities in a financial model. In other words, if the price of an underlying variable changes, the price of an output does not change linearly, but depends on the second derivative (or, loosely speaking, higher-order terms) of the modeling function. Geometrically, the model is no longer flat but curved, and the degree of curvature is called the convexity.

Payback period in capital budgeting refers to the time required to recoup the funds expended in an investment, or to reach the break-even point. An initial public offering (IPO) or stock launch is a public offering in which shares of a company are sold to institutional investors and usually also to retail investors. An IPO is typically underwritten by one or more investment banks, who also arrange for the shares to be listed on one or more stock exchanges. Corporate governance refers to the mechanisms, processes, practices, and relations by which corporations are controlled and operated by their boards of directors, managers, shareholders, and stakeholders.

Bankruptcy is a legal process through which people or other entities who cannot repay debts to creditors may seek relief from some or all of their debts. In most jurisdictions, bankruptcy is imposed by a court order, often initiated by the debtor. Bankrupt is not the only legal status that an insolvent person may have, meaning the term bankruptcy is not a synonym for insolvency.

Dupont analysis is a tool used in financial analysis, where return on equity (ROE) is separated into its component parts. Useful in several contexts, this "decomposition" of ROE allows financial managers to focus on the key metrics of financial performance individually, and thereby to identify strengths and weaknesses within the company that should be addressed. Similarly, it allows investors to compare the operational efficiency of two comparable firms.

The Z-score formula for predicting bankruptcy was published in 1968 by Edward I. Altman, who was, at the time, an Assistant Professor of Finance at New York University. The formula may be used to determine the probability that a firm will go into bankruptcy within two years. Tax authorities will generally view a person as self-employed if the person chooses to be recognised as such or if the person is generating income for which a tax return needs to be filed. A value-added tax (VAT or goods and services tax (GST), general consumption tax (GCT)) is a consumption tax that is levied on the value added at each stage of a product's production and distribution. VAT is similar to, and is often compared with, a sales tax. VAT is an indirect tax because individuals do not pay it directly to the government; instead, suppliers act as intermediaries by collecting the tax from customers at the point of sale and remitting it to the government. The Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is the current tax depreciation system in the United States. Under this system, the capitalized cost (basis) of tangible property is recovered over a specified life by annual deductions for depreciation. Form W-2, officially titled the "Wage and Tax Statement", is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) tax form used in the United States to report wages paid to employees and the federal taxes withheld from their pay. Employers are required to complete a Form W-2 for each employee who receives salary, wages, or other compensation in the context of an employment relationship. Form 1040, officially titled the U.S. Individual Income Tax Return, is an IRS tax form used by United States residents to file personal federal income tax returns. The form is used to calculate a taxpayer's total taxable income and to determine the amount of tax owed to or refundable from the federal government. Liabilities are usually segregated into current liabilities and long-term liabilities, where current liabilities include anything expected to be settled within one year of the balance sheet date. The asset information on the balance sheet can be combined with the sales line item on the income statement to estimate the efficiency with which a business is using its assets to produce sales. For example, the asset turnover ratio shows the efficiency of asset usage by dividing average total assets by net sales. Similarly, net working capital can be compared to sales to estimate the efficiency of working capital usage.

The return generated by a business can be calculated by dividing the net income figure on the income statement by the shareholders’ equity figure on the balance sheet. A variation on the concept is to divide net income by the total assets figure on the balance sheet. Either approach is used by investors to determine the rate of return being generated.

response: Cost of goods sold is the total of all costs used to create a product or service, which has been sold. These costs fall into the general sub-categories of direct labor, direct materials, and overhead. Direct labor and direct materials are variable costs, while overhead is comprised of fixed costs (such as utilities, rent, and supervisory salaries). Under the first in, first out method (FIFO), the cost of the first unit to enter inventory is charged to expense first. In an inflationary environment, the least expensive (oldest) inventory items are charged to expense first, which tends to inflate the reported profit level. It also means that the ending inventory level is at its highest. This approach typically reflects actual usage patterns.

All supplier invoices are immediately routed to the payables department as soon as they are received. This can be a difficult processing step, since invoices might have been sent to the person authorizing a purchase, or perhaps to a subsidiary. In either case, there must be a firm requirement for the recipient to immediately forward the invoice to the payables department. A particular concern is when invoices are sent to people who no longer work for the company - perhaps by email; if so, it ...

Each received invoice should be examined to verify whether the company actually owes the indicated amount, as well as to determine whether it contains the correct unit quantities, unit prices, and payment date. If not, the payables department must contact the supplier to request that a corrected invoice be sent. The department may also compare the invoice to the authorizing purchase order to ensure that the delivery was authorized, and compare it to receiving documentation to ensure that the ...

Once the preceding step has been completed, the invoice is recorded in the company’s accounting system, using the invoice date as the entry date. The payment date is based on the invoice date. For example, if an invoice has a date of September 1 and should be paid in 30 days, then it is logged in as of September 1, so that the accounting system will pay it on September 30.

On each scheduled payment date, the accountant runs a preliminary check register and reviews it to ensure that all stated payments should be made. If not, they are flagged to be paid at a later date. The remaining payments are made, using either checks or electronic payments. Depending on the controls used, these payments may need to be approved before they are issued.

Accounts receivable may be further subdivided into trade receivables and non trade receivables, where trade receivables are from a company's normal business partners, and non trade receivables are all other receivables, such as amounts due from employees. The amount of non trade receivables is usually quite small. Goodwill is the excess of the purchase price paid for an acquired entity and the amount of the price not assigned to acquired assets and liabilities. It arises when an acquirer pays a high price to acquire another business. This asset only arises from an acquisition; it cannot be generated internally.

A contingent liability is a potential obligation that may arise depending on the outcome of a future event. The obligation is uncertain because it depends on circumstances that have not yet been resolved. Common examples include pending lawsuits, loan guarantees, and product warranties. Depending on the likelihood and measurability of the obligation, it may be disclosed or recognized in the financial statements.

patterns: how do stock splits affect earnings per share
response: A stock split increases the number of outstanding shares while proportionally reducing the par value or price per share, but it does not change the company’s total earnings. As a result, earnings per share decreases after the split because the same net income is spread across more shares. This adjustment ensures EPS remains comparable across different reporting periods despite the change in share count.

Sales taxes are regressive, because they have a larger impact on low-income people than high-income people. Nearly all of the income earned by a low-income person is used to make purchases, on which sales taxes are imposed. This is not the case for high-income people, who put a large part of their earnings into investments, and avoid paying sales taxes by doing so.

Most accrued liabilities are created as reversing entries, so that the accounting software automatically cancels them in the following period. This happens when you are expecting supplier invoices to arrive in the next period. A best practice is to reverse them in the following period automatically under all circumstances, simply to make sure that the initial entry is flushed out of the books every month. Otherwise, there is a risk that an accrued liability will linger on the books for an ext...

patterns: how does industry structure affect acceptable ranges for the current ratio
response: Industry structure influences acceptable current ratio ranges because operating models and cash cycles differ. Retailers often operate with lower ratios due to rapid inventory turnover and strong cash inflows, while capital-intensive or manufacturing firms require higher ratios to support inventory and receivable balances, ensuring sufficient liquidity to meet short-term obligations.

patterns: is direct labor a variable cost
response: Direct labor may not be a variable cost if labor is not added to or subtracted from the production process as production volumes change. This situation arises when a production line must be fully staffed, irrespective of the amount of production volume. This is a common situation in large and complex assembly lines, where all positions must be staffed before operations can commence.

A fixed cost is a cost that does not increase or decrease in conjunction with any activities. It must be paid by an organization on a recurring basis, even if there is no business activity. The amount charged to expense tends to change little from period to period. Fixed costs tend to be incurred on a regular basis, and so are considered to be period costs. The concept is used in financial analysis to find the breakeven point of a business, as well as to determine product pricing.

Job costing involves the accumulation of the costs of materials, labor, and overhead for a specific job. This approach is an excellent tool for tracing specific costs to individual jobs and examining them to see if the costs can be reduced in later jobs. An alternative use is to see if any excess costs incurred can be billed to a customer. Job costing is used to accumulate costs at a small-unit level. patterns: step 5 allocate costs in secondary pools to primary pools
response: Use activity drivers to reassign the costs accumulated in the secondary cost pools to the primary cost pools that consume those services. For example, allocate maintenance based on machine hours, information technology based on user counts, and human resources based on employee headcount. patterns: step 6 charge costs to cost objects
response: Use an activity driver to allocate the contents of each primary cost pool to cost objects. There will be a separate activity driver for each cost pool. To allocate the costs, divide the total cost in each cost pool by the total amount of activity in the activity driver, to establish the cost per unit of activity. Then allocate the cost per unit to the cost objects, based on their use of the activity driver.

patterns: what types of businesses benefit from activity-based costing
response: Businesses with diverse products, multiple processes, or significant overhead benefit most from activity-based costing. Examples include manufacturers with varied product lines, hospitals, logistics companies, and service firms with complex client work. ABC is most useful when traditional costing spreads overhead too broadly and obscures the true cost of activities.

A variance is the difference between the actual cost incurred and the standard cost against which it is measured. A variance can also be used to measure the difference between actual and expected sales. Thus, variance analysis can be used to review the performance of both revenue and expenses. There are two basic types of variances from a standard that can arise, which are the rate variance and the volume variance. patterns: what limitations arise when standard costing is used in lean environments
response: In lean environments, detailed standard cost variances can emphasize local efficiency over end-to-end process flow and waste reduction. Frequent variance reporting may also encourage overproduction or inventory buildup to absorb fixed costs, which conflicts with lean principles. As a result, standard costing often provides less decision-useful information than value stream or operational performance measures in lean settings.

patterns: how is the master budget used for performance evaluation
response: The master budget is used for performance evaluation by comparing actual financial results to budgeted targets. Variances between planned and actual figures help management identify areas of efficiency or underperformance. This analysis supports accountability, motivates managers, and guides corrective actions to improve future operations.

patterns: what is the difference between authorized issued and outstanding common stock
response: Authorized common stock is the maximum number of shares a corporation is legally permitted to issue under its charter. Issued shares are the portion of authorized stock that the company has actually distributed to shareholders. Outstanding shares represent issued shares currently held by investors, excluding any treasury stock repurchased by the company.

Sales Revenue is the company’s revenue from sales or services, displayed at the very top of the statement. This value will be the gross of the costs associated with creating the goods sold or in providing services. Some companies have multiple revenue streams that add to a total revenue line.

Most businesses have some expenses related to selling goods and/or services. Marketing, advertising, and promotion expenses are often grouped together as they are similar expenses, all related to selling.

SG&A Expenses include the selling, general, and administrative section that contains all other indirect costs associated with running the business. This includes salaries and wages, rent and office expenses, insurance, travel expenses, and sometimes depreciation and amortization, along with other operational expenses. Entities may, however, elect to separate depreciation and amortization in their own section.

While not present in all income statements, EBITDA stands for Earnings before Interest, Tax, Depreciation, and Amortization. It is calculated by subtracting SG&A expenses (excluding amortization and depreciation) from gross profit.

Businesses often have other expenses that are unique to their industry. Other expenses may include fulfillment, technology, research and development (R&D), stock-based compensation (SBC), impairment charges, gains/losses on the sale of investments, foreign exchange impacts, and many other expenses that are industry or company-specific.

Forecast specific line items, and use these to calculate subtotals. For example, for future gross profit, it is better to forecast COGS and revenue and subtract them from each other, rather than to forecast future gross profit directly.

Companies benchmark their ROA against competitors to gauge relative efficiency and identify best practices worth adopting across their own operations.

response: Comparing financial ratios with competitors or industry benchmarks helps analysts to determine a company’s relative performance. These comparisons indicate whether the business is stronger, weaker, or on par with peers, guiding management in identifying competitive advantages and areas for improvement.

In finance, subordinated debt (also known as subordinated loan, subordinated bond, subordinated debenture or junior debt) is debt which ranks after other debts if a company falls into liquidation or bankruptcy. In finance, senior debt is debt that takes priority over other unsecured or otherwise more "junior" debt owed by an issuer. patterns: can a change in estimate be reversed
response: A change in estimate can be reversed if new information shows that the revised estimate is no longer accurate. The reversal is applied prospectively, affecting only the current and future periods, without restating prior financial statements. This ensures that reported figures reflect the most current and reliable information available.

patterns: how does a restatement differ from a revision
response: A restatement corrects material errors and requires reissuance of previously issued financial statements. A revision corrects immaterial errors by adjusting current-period statements without reissuing prior financials. The distinction is driven by the materiality of the error.

patterns: what types of assets commonly generate unrealized gains
response: Assets that commonly generate unrealized gains include marketable equity and debt securities measured at fair value, investment real estate subject to revaluation, and derivative financial instruments. Foreign currency–denominated assets may also produce unrealized gains due to exchange rate movements. These gains arise from changes in market prices, interest rates, or valuation assumptions rather than completed transactions.

A realized gain occurs when the sale price of an asset is higher than its carrying amount. This gain is only considered to be realized when the asset is removed from the entity's accounting records. Thus, a gain is only realized when the associated asset has been sold, donated, or scrapped. Realistically, a gain only arises when an asset is sold, since there is no income associated with a scrapped asset. Unrealized gains are generally not taxed, while realized gains are taxed.

patterns: how does other comprehensive income affect shareholders equity
response: Other comprehensive income affects shareholders’ equity through the accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI) account, which captures unrealized gains and losses not yet included in net income. These changes adjust total equity without impacting retained earnings until the underlying items are realized. As a result, other comprehensive income provides a bridge between current performance and future potential impacts on owners’ equity.

patterns: how is the dividend payout ratio different from the retention ratio

Mathematically, the retention ratio equals 1 minus the payout ratio. Together, they explain how a company balances rewarding shareholders with reinvesting in future growth.

Spoilage is waste or scrap arising from the production process. It may also be caused by improper materials handling and storage, which render goods unusable. The term is most commonly applied to raw materials that have a short life span, such as food used in the restaurant industry.

Scrap is the excess unusable material that is left over after a product has been manufactured. This residual amount has minimal value, and is usually sold off for its material content. A business can reduce the amount of scrap that it generates by exercising great care in setting up production equipment, buying raw materials of adequate quality, and training employees in the proper use of production equipment.

patterns: how does the reciprocal method differ from the direct method
response: The reciprocal method differs from the direct method by recognizing all mutual services exchanged between service departments. In contrast, the direct method ignores interdepartmental services and allocates costs only to production departments. As a result, the reciprocal method provides a more accurate and comprehensive allocation of overhead costs.

patterns: how do temporary differences affect financial statements

These differences reverse over time as the underlying assets are recovered or liabilities are settled.

Part of the calculation is to ensure the full repayment of principal, so to the extent that it’s possible, a lender will always seek to exert any influence on that borrower to help ensure financial well-being. This is where loan covenants come in. By expressly requiring or restricting specific actions or circumstances, loan covenants serve two primary purposes. The first is to help align incentives between the borrower and the lender; the se...

An example in commercial lending is where a management team may wish to distribute a large proportion of company earnings to shareholders through dividend payments. The lender would rather see that cash retained within the corporation to provide a cash buffer or, perhaps, to repay some of its outstanding credit. This misalignment of incentives can be managed by limiting or restricting dividends or by requiring that financial covenant calculations be net of dividend payments. The most basic il...

Loan covenants are also used to mitigate specific risks. Think about a borrower taking on high leverage (or gearing) to make an acquisition. It’s in the lender’s best interest to have that borrower deleverage as quickly as possible to bring its leverage ratios back to long-term expectations or industry norms.
